This helps with debugging.</p> <div class="sourceCode" id="cb560"><pre class="sourceCode python"><code class="sourceCode python"><span id="cb560-1"><a href="#cb560-1" aria-hidden="true" tabindex="-1"></a><span class="im">import</span> math</span> <span id="cb560-2"><a href="#cb560-2" aria-hidden="true" tabindex="-1"></a><span class="im">from</span> math <span class="im">import</span> sqrt</span> <span id="cb560-3"><a href="#cb560-3" aria-hidden="true" tabindex="-1"></a><span class="im">from</span> math <span class="im">import</span> pi</span> <span id="cb560-4"><a href="#cb560-4" aria-hidden="true" tabindex="-1"></a><span class="im">from</span> math <span class="im">import</span> sqrt, pi</span> <span id="cb560-5"><a href="#cb560-5" aria-hidden="true" tabindex="-1"></a><span class="im">from</span> math <span class="im">import</span> <span class="op">*</span></span> <span id="cb560-6"><a href="#cb560-6" aria-hidden="true" tabindex="-1"></a><span class="im">from</span> math <span class="im">import</span> <span class="op">*</span> </span></code></pre></div> </section> <section id="finding-out-what-is-in-a-module" class="level1"> <h1>Finding Out What Is in a Module</h1> <p>The contents of any module can be examined with dir() and help().</p> <div class="sourceCode" id="cb561"><pre class="sourceCode txt"><code class="sourceCode default"><span id="cb561-1"><a href="#cb561-1" aria-hidden="true" tabindex="-1"></a>dir(math) Shows everything inside the module </span> <span id="cb561-2"><a href="#cb561-2" aria-hidden="true" tabindex="-1"></a>dir() Shows everything in the global namespace </span> <span id="cb561-3"><a href="#cb561-3" aria-hidden="true" tabindex="-1"></a>help(math.sqrt) Displays the help text of a module or function </span> <span id="cb561-4"><a href="#cb561-4" aria-hidden="true" tabindex="-1"></a>__name__ Name of a module </span> <span id="cb561-5"><a href="#cb561-5" aria-hidden="true" tabindex="-1"></a>__doc__ Help text of a module </span> <span id="cb561-6"><a href="#cb561-6" aria-hidden="true" tabindex="-1"></a>__builtin__ Container with all standard Python functions </span></code></pre></div> </section> <section id="where-python-looks-for-modules" class="level1"> <h1>Where Python Looks for Modules</h1> <p>When importing modules or packages, Python looks in</p> <ul> <li><p>the current directory,<br /> </p></li> <li><p>the Python2.6/lib/site-packages folder, and</p></li> <li><p>everything in the PYTHONPATH environment variable. In Python, it can be accessed with</p></li> </ul> <div class="sourceCode" id="cb562"><pre class="sourceCode python"><code class="sourceCode python"><span id="cb562-1"><a href="#cb562-1" aria-hidden="true" tabindex="-1"></a><span class="im">import</span> sys </span> <span id="cb562-2"><a href="#cb562-2" aria-hidden="true" tabindex="-1"></a><span class="bu">print</span> sys.path </span> <span id="cb562-3"><a href="#cb562-3" aria-hidden="true" tabindex="-1"></a>sys.path.append(<span class="st">'my_directory'</span>) </span> <span id="cb562-4"><a href="#cb562-4" aria-hidden="true" tabindex="-1"></a><span class="im">import</span> mypackage.my_module </span></code></pre></div> </section> <section id="packages" class="level1"> <h1>Packages</h1> <p>For very big programs, you might find it useful to divide the Python code into several directories. There are two things to keep in mind when doing that:</p> <ul> <li>To import the package from outside, you need to ensure a file __ init__.py (it may be empty) is in the package directory.<br /> </li> <li>The directory with the package needs to be in the Python search path (see above).</li> </ul> </section> <section id="a.2.13-input-and-output" class="level1"> <h1>A.2.13 Input and Output</h1> <p>Reading Text from the Keyboard into a Variable</p> <p>User input can be read from the keyboard with or without a message text:</p> <div class="sourceCode" id="cb563"><pre class="sourceCode python"><code class="sourceCode python"><span id="cb563-1"><a href="#cb563-1" aria-hidden="true" tabindex="-1"></a>a <span class="op">=</span> <span class="bu">raw_input</span>() Reads text <span class="im">from</span> the keyboard to a string variable </span> <span id="cb563-2"><a href="#cb563-2" aria-hidden="true" tabindex="-1"></a>a <span class="op">=</span> <span class="bu">raw_input</span>(<span class="st">'please Enter a number'</span>) Displays the text, then reads a string <span class="im">from</span> the keyboard </span></code></pre></div> </section> <section id="printing-text" class="level1"> <h1>Printing Text</h1> <p>The Python print statement writes text to the console where Python was started. The print command is very versatile and accepts almost any combination of strings, numbers, function calls, and arithmetic operations separated by commas. By default, print generates a newline character at the end.</p> <div class="sourceCode" id="cb564"><pre class="sourceCode txt"><code class="sourceCode default"><span id="cb564-1"><a href="#cb564-1" aria-hidden="true" tabindex="-1"></a>print('Hello World') Displays a text </span> <span id="cb564-2"><a href="#cb564-2" aria-hidden="true" tabindex="-1"></a>print 3.4 Displays the number </span> <span id="cb564-3"><a href="#cb564-3" aria-hidden="true" tabindex="-1"></a>print 3 + 4 Displays the result of the calculation </span> <span id="cb564-4"><a href="#cb564-4" aria-hidden="true" tabindex="-1"></a>print a Displays the contents of the variable a </span> <span id="cb564-5"><a href="#cb564-5" aria-hidden="true" tabindex="-1"></a>print('')'line one line two line three''' Displays text stretching over multiple lines </span> <span id="cb564-6"><a href="#cb564-6" aria-hidden="true" tabindex="-1"></a>print('number'), 77 Displays the text, a tab, and the number </span></code></pre></div> <p>print int(a) * 7</p> <p>print</p> <p>Displays the result of the multiplication after converting the variable to an integer</p> <p>Displays an empty line</p> </section> <section id="string-formatting" class="level1"> <h1>String Formatting</h1> <p>Variables and strings can be combined using formatting characters. This works also within a print statement. In both cases, the number of values and formatting characters must be equal.</p> <div class="sourceCode" id="cb565"><pre class="sourceCode txt"><code class="sourceCode default"><span id="cb565-1"><a href="#cb565-1" aria-hidden="true" tabindex="-1"></a>s = 'Result:%i%' (number) </span> <span id="cb565-2"><a href="#cb565-2" aria-hidden="true" tabindex="-1"></a>print('Hello%s!') ('Roger') </span> <span id="cb565-3"><a href="#cb565-3" aria-hidden="true" tabindex="-1"></a>print('(6.3f/%6.3f)')%(a,b) </span></code></pre></div> <p>The formatting characters include the following:</p> <ul> <li>%i: an integer<br /> </li> <li>%4i: an integer formatted to length 4<br /> </li> <li>%6.2f: a float number with length 6 and 2 after the comma<br /> </li> <li>%10s: a right-oriented string with length 10</li> </ul> </section> <section id="reading-and-writing-files" class="level1"> <h1>Reading and Writing Files</h1> <p>Text files can be accessed using the open() function. It returns an open file whose contents can be extracted as a string or strings that can be written. If you try to open a file that does not exist, an IOError will be created.</p> <div class="sourceCode" id="cb566"><pre class="sourceCode python"><code class="sourceCode python"><span id="cb566-1"><a href="#cb566-1" aria-hidden="true" tabindex="-1"></a>f <span class="op">=</span> <span class="bu">open</span>(<span class="st">'my_file.txt'</span>) Reads a text <span class="bu">file</span> <span class="kw">and</span> its contents into a </span> <span id="cb566-2"><a href="#cb566-2" aria-hidden="true" tabindex="-1"></a>text <span class="op">=</span> f.read() string variable </span> <span id="cb566-3"><a href="#cb566-3" aria-hidden="true" tabindex="-1"></a>f <span class="op">=</span> <span class="bu">open</span>(<span class="st">'my_file.txt'</span>, <span class="st">'w'</span>) Creates a new text <span class="bu">file</span> <span class="kw">and</span> writes text <span class="im">from</span> </span> <span id="cb566-4"><a href="#cb566-4" aria-hidden="true" tabindex="-1"></a>f.write(text) a string variable into it </span> <span id="cb566-5"><a href="#cb566-5" aria-hidden="true" tabindex="-1"></a>f <span class="op">=</span> <span class="bu">open</span>(<span class="st">'my_file.txt'</span>, <span class="st">'a'</span>) Appends text to an already existing <span class="bu">file</span> </span> <span id="cb566-6"><a href="#cb566-6" aria-hidden="true" tabindex="-1"></a>f.write(text) </span> <span id="cb566-7"><a href="#cb566-7" aria-hidden="true" tabindex="-1"></a>f.close() Closes a <span class="bu">file</span> after usage<span class="op">;</span> closing <span class="kw">in</span> Python </span> <span id="cb566-8"><a href="#cb566-8" aria-hidden="true" tabindex="-1"></a>lines <span class="op">=</span> f.readlines() <span class="kw">is</span> good style but <span class="kw">not</span> always mandatory </span> <span id="cb566-9"><a href="#cb566-9" aria-hidden="true" tabindex="-1"></a>f.writelines lines) Reads <span class="bu">all</span> lines <span class="im">from</span> a text <span class="bu">file</span> to a <span class="bu">list</span> </span> <span id="cb566-10"><a href="#cb566-10" aria-hidden="true" tabindex="-1"></a><span class="cf">for</span> line <span class="kw">in</span> <span class="bu">open</span>(name): Goes through <span class="bu">all</span> lines <span class="kw">and</span> prints each line </span> <span id="cb566-11"><a href="#cb566-11" aria-hidden="true" tabindex="-1"></a><span class="bu">print</span>(line)</span> <span id="cb566-12"><a href="#cb566-12" aria-hidden="true" tabindex="-1"></a>lines <span class="op">=</span> [<span class="st">'first line</span><span class="ch">\n</span><span class="st">'</span>, <span class="st">'second'</span> creates a <span class="bu">list</span> of lines <span class="cf">with</span> newline </span> <span id="cb566-13"><a href="#cb566-13" aria-hidden="true" tabindex="-1"></a>line\n<span class="st">'] characters at the end and saves it to a </span></span> <span id="cb566-14"><a href="#cb566-14" aria-hidden="true" tabindex="-1"></a><span class="er">f = open</span>(<span class="st">'my_file.txt'</span>, <span class="st">'w'</span>) text <span class="bu">file</span> </span> <span id="cb566-15"><a href="#cb566-15" aria-hidden="true" tabindex="-1"></a>f.writelines (lines) </span></code></pre></div> </section> <section id="directory-names-in-python" class="level1"> <h1>Directory Names in Python</h1> <p>When opening files, you can also use full or relative directory names. However, on Windows, you must replace the backslash <span class="math inline">\(^ { \alpha } \backslash ^ { \gamma }\)</span> with a double backslash <span class="math inline">\(^ { \alpha } \backslash \backslash ^ { \beta }\)</span> (because “” is also used for <span class="math inline">\(^ { \alpha } \backslash \boldsymbol { \mathrm { n } } ^ { \gamma }\)</span> and <span class="math inline">\(^ { \alpha } \backslash \ t ^ { \gamma } )\)</span> .</p> <div class="sourceCode" id="cb567"><pre class="sourceCode python"><code class="sourceCode python"><span id="cb567-1"><a href="#cb567-1" aria-hidden="true" tabindex="-1"></a>f <span class="op">=</span> <span class="bu">open</span>(<span class="st">'..</span><span class="er">\</span><span class="st">my_file.txt'</span>)</span> <span id="cb567-2"><a href="#cb567-2" aria-hidden="true" tabindex="-1"></a>f <span class="op">=</span> <span class="bu">open</span>(<span class="st">'C:</span><span class="er">\</span><span class="st">python</span><span class="er">\</span><span class="st">my_file.txt'</span>) </span></code></pre></div> </section> <section id="the-csv-module" class="level1"> <h1>The csv Module</h1> <p>An open file behaves like a list of strings. Thus, it is possible to read it line by line using the csv module:</p> <div class="sourceCode" id="cb568"><pre class="sourceCode txt"><code class="sourceCode default"><span id="cb568-1"><a href="#cb568-1" aria-hidden="true" tabindex="-1"></a>>>> import csv</span> <span id="cb568-2"><a href="#cb568-2" aria-hidden="true" tabindex="-1"></a>>>> reader = csv_reader(open('RSMB_HUMAN.fasta'))</span> <span id="cb568-3"><a href="#cb568-3" aria-hidden="true" tabindex="-1"></a>>>> for row in reader: print(row)</span> <span id="cb568-4"><a href="#cb568-4" aria-hidden="true" tabindex="-1"></a>...</span> <span id="cb568-5"><a href="#cb568-5" aria-hidden="true" tabindex="-1"></a>['>gi|4507127|ref|NP_003084.1|U1 small nuclear ribonucleoprotein C [Homo sapiens]']</span> <span id="cb568-6"><a href="#cb568-6" aria-hidden="true" tabindex="-1"></a>['MPFKFYCDYCDTYLTHDSPSVRKTHCSGRKHK ENVKDYYQKWMEQQAQSLLIDKTTAFQQGKPPTPFSAP']</span> <span id="cb568-7"><a href="#cb568-7" aria-hidden="true" tabindex="-1"></a>['PPAGAMIPPPPPLPGPPRPGMMPAPHMGGPP MPPMMGPPPPGMMPVGPAPGMRRPMGGHMPMMPGPPMMR']</span> <span id="cb568-8"><a href="#cb568-8" aria-hidden="true" tabindex="-1"></a>['PPARPMMVPTRPGMTRPDR']</span> <span id="cb568-9"><a href="#cb568-9" aria-hidden="true" tabindex="-1"></a>[ ] </span></code></pre></div> <p>Similarly, lists and tuples can be written to files using csv:</p> <div class="sourceCode" id="cb569"><pre class="sourceCode python"><code class="sourceCode python"><span id="cb569-1"><a href="#cb569-1" aria-hidden="true" tabindex="-1"></a><span class="op">>>></span> <span class="im">import</span> csv</span> <span id="cb569-2"><a href="#cb569-2" aria-hidden="true" tabindex="-1"></a><span class="op">>>></span> table <span class="op">=</span> [[<span class="dv">1</span>,<span class="dv">2</span>,<span class="dv">3</span>],[<span class="dv">4</span>,<span class="dv">5</span>,<span class="dv">6</span>]]</span> <span id="cb569-3"><a href="#cb569-3" aria-hidden="true" tabindex="-1"></a><span class="op">>>></span> writer <span class="op">=</span> csv.writer(<span class="bu">open</span>(<span class="st">'my_file.csv'</span>, <span class="st">'w'</span>))</span> <span id="cb569-4"><a href="#cb569-4" aria-hidden="true" tabindex="-1"></a><span class="op">>>></span> writer.writerow(table) </span></code></pre></div> </section> <section id="options-of-csv-file-readerswriters" class="level1"> <h1>Options of csv File Readers/Writers</h1> <p>Both csv readers and writers can handle a lot of different formats. Options you can change include the following:</p> <ul> <li>delimiter: the symbol separating columns<br /> </li> <li>quotechar: the symbol used to quote strings<br /> </li> <li>lineterminator: the symbol at the end of lines</li> </ul> <p>reader <span class="math inline">\(=\)</span> csv-reader(open(‘my_file.csv’),delimiter <span class="math inline">\(= \mathrm{''\backslash t''}\)</span> ，quotechar <span class="math inline">\(= \mathrm{''''}\)</span> ）</p> </section> <section id="a.2.14-managing-directories" class="level1"> <h1>A.2.14 Managing Directories</h1> <p>With the os module, you can change to a different directory:</p> <div class="sourceCode" id="cb570"><pre class="sourceCode python"><code class="sourceCode python"><span id="cb570-1"><a href="#cb570-1" aria-hidden="true" tabindex="-1"></a><span class="im">import</span> os</span> <span id="cb570-2"><a href="#cb570-2" aria-hidden="true" tabindex="-1"></a>os.chdir(<span class="st">'..</span><span class="er">\</span><span class="st">python'</span>) </span></code></pre></div> <p>You can also get a list with all files:</p> <div class="sourceCode" id="cb571"><pre class="sourceCode javascript"><code class="sourceCode javascript"><span id="cb571-1"><a href="#cb571-1" aria-hidden="true" tabindex="-1"></a>os<span class="op">.</span><span class="fu">listdir</span>(<span class="st">'..</span><span class="sc">\p</span><span class="st">ython'</span>) </span></code></pre></div> <p>The third most important function is for checking whether a file exists:</p> <div class="sourceCode" id="cb572"><pre class="sourceCode txt"><code class="sourceCode default"><span id="cb572-1"><a href="#cb572-1" aria-hidden="true" tabindex="-1"></a>print os.access('my_file.txt', os.F_OK) </span></code></pre></div> </section> <section id="a.2.15-getting-the-current-time-and-date" class="level1"> <h1>A.2.15 Getting the Current Time and Date</h1> <p>The time module offers functions for getting the current time and date:</p> <div class="sourceCode" id="cb573"><pre class="sourceCode python"><code class="sourceCode python"><span id="cb573-1"><a href="#cb573-1" aria-hidden="true" tabindex="-1"></a><span class="im">import</span> time </span> <span id="cb573-2"><a href="#cb573-2" aria-hidden="true" tabindex="-1"></a>s <span class="op">=</span> time.asftime() <span class="co"># as string </span></span> <span id="cb573-3"><a href="#cb573-3" aria-hidden="true" tabindex="-1"></a>i <span class="op">=</span> time.time() <span class="co"># as float </span></span></code></pre></div> </section> <section id="a.2.16-accessing-web-pages" class="level1"> <h1>A.2.16 Accessing Web Pages</h1> <p>You can access the HTML code of web pages and downloadable files from the web in a way similar to the way you read files:</p> <p>import urllib<br /> url <span class="math inline">\(=\)</span> ‘http://www.google.com’<br /> page <span class="math inline">\(=\)</span> urllib(urlopen(url)<br /> print page.read()</p> </section> <section id="a.2.17-regular-expressions" class="level1"> <h1>A.2.17 Regular Expressions</h1> <p>Regular expressions allow performing pattern matching on strings and search and replace text in a sophisticated way:</p> <div class="sourceCode" id="cb574"><pre class="sourceCode python"><code class="sourceCode python"><span id="cb574-1"><a href="#cb574-1" aria-hidden="true" tabindex="-1"></a><span class="im">import</span> re</span> <span id="cb574-2"><a href="#cb574-2" aria-hidden="true" tabindex="-1"></a>text <span class="op">=</span> <span class="st">'all ways lead to Rome'</span> </span></code></pre></div> <p>Searching Whether Text Exists</p> <p>re.search(‘R…’, text)</p> <p>Finding All Words</p> <p>re.findall(‘(.o)’, text)</p> <p>Replacing</p> <p>re.sub(‘R[meo]+’,‘London’, text)</p> <p>How to Find the Right Pattern for Your Problem</p> <p>Finding the right Regex requires lots of trial-and-error search. You can test regular expressions online before including them into your program:</p> <p>http://www.regexplanet.com/simple/</p> <p>Characters Used in Regex Patterns</p> <p>Some of the most commonly used characters in Regular Expression (RE) patterns are as follows:</p> <table> <tr> <td> Character </td> <td> Action </td> </tr> <tr> <td> /td> <td> Match decimal character [0..9] </td> </tr> <tr> <td> </td> <td> Match alphanumeric [a..z] or [0..9] </td> </tr> <tr> <td> </td> <td> Match at the start of the text </td> </tr> <tr> <td> </td> <td> Match at the end of the text </td> </tr> <tr> <td> [ABC] </td> <td> Match one of characters A,B,C </td> </tr> <tr> <td> . </td> <td> Match any character (wildcard) </td> </tr> <tr> <td> ^A </td> <td> Match any character but A </td> </tr> <tr> <td> a+ </td> <td> Match one or more of pattern a </td> </tr> <tr> <td> a* </td> <td> Match zero or more of pattern a </td> </tr> <tr> <td> a|b </td> <td> Match either pattern a or b </td> </tr> <tr> <td> (a) </td> <td> re.findall() returns a </td> </tr> <tr> <td> </td> <td> Match an empty space </td> </tr> </table> </section> <section id="re-module-methods" class="level1"> <h1>re Module Methods</h1> <table> <tr> <td> Method </td> <td> Returned Object </td> <td> Attribute Of </td> <td> Function </td> </tr> <tr> <td> compile() </td> <td> Regex object </td> <td> </td> <td> Compiles a RE </td> </tr> <tr> <td> match() </td> <td> Match object </td> <td> Regex object </td> <td> Determines if the RE matches at the beginning of the string </td> </tr> <tr> <td> search() </td> <td> Match object </td> <td> Regex object </td> <td> Scans through a string, looking for any location where the RE matches </td> </tr> <tr> <td> findall() </td> <td> List </td> <td> Regex object </td> <td> Finds all substrings where the RE matches and returns them as a list </td> </tr> <tr> <td> finditer() </td> <td> Iterator object </td> <td> Regex object </td> <td> Finds all substrings where the RE matches and returns them as an iterator </td> </tr> <tr> <td> span() </td> <td> Tuple </td> <td> Match object </td> <td> Returns a tuple containing the (start, end) positions of the match </td> </tr> <tr> <td> start() </td> <td> Integer </td> <td> Match object </td> <td> Returns the starting position of the match </td> </tr> <tr> <td> end() </td> <td> Integer </td> <td> Match object </td> <td> Returns the ending position of the match </td> </tr> <tr> <td> group() </td> <td> String </td> <td> Match object </td> <td> Returns the string matched by the RE </td> </tr> <tr> <td> groups() </td> <td> Tuple </td> <td> Match object </td> <td> Returns a tuple containing the strings for all the subgroups </td> </tr> <tr> <td> split(s) </td> <td> List </td> <td> Regex object </td> <td> Splits the string into a list, splitting it wherever the RE matches </td> </tr> <tr> <td> sub(r, s) </td> <td> String </td> <td> Regex object </td> <td> Finds all substrings where the RE matches and replaces them with a different string </td> </tr> <tr> <td> subn(r, s) </td> <td> Tuple </td> <td> Regex object </td> <td> Does the same thing as sub() but returns the new string and the number of replacements </td> </tr> </table> </section> <section id="regular-expression-compilation-flags" class="level1"> <h1>Regular Expression Compilation Flags</h1> <p>You can customize pattern matching by adding an argument to the re.compile() function. For example, if you want to perform a caseinsensitive search, you can add re.IGNORECASE to the re.compile() arguments.</p> <p>This is a list of the possible options that you can use to customize your search:</p> <table> <tr> <td> Flag </td> <td> Function </td> </tr> <tr> <td> DOTALL, S </td> <td> Makes a dot (.) match any character, including newlines </td> </tr> <tr> <td> IGNORECASE, I </td> <td> Does case-insensitive matches </td> </tr> <tr> <td> LOCALE, L </td> <td> Does a locale-aware match, i.e., takes into account C libraries for local systems (e.g., other languages) </td> </tr> <tr> <td> MULTILINE, M </td> <td> Does multiline matching, affecting ^ and <span class="math inline">\(: ^ (\)</span>) matches at the beginning (end) of each line in a string </td> </tr> <tr> <td> VERBOSE, V </td> <td> Enables verbose REs, which can be organized more cleanly and understandably, i.e., is allowed to write a RE in a more readable way (e.g., by introducing whitespaces or comments) </td> </tr> </table> </section> <section id="a.2.18-debugging" class="level1"> <h1>A.2.18 Debugging</h1> </section> <section id="exceptions" class="level1"> <h1>Exceptions</h1> <p>No matter how good your program is, sometimes things will go wrong. However, you can make sure it doesn’t blow up in the wrong moment, so that, e.g., important data can be saved. Exceptions are the Python mechanisms that let you know that something went wrong.</p> <div class="sourceCode" id="cb575"><pre class="sourceCode python"><code class="sourceCode python"><span id="cb575-1"><a href="#cb575-1" aria-hidden="true" tabindex="-1"></a>a <span class="op">=</span> <span class="bu">raw_input</span>(<span class="st">'enter a number'</span>) </span> <span id="cb575-2"><a href="#cb575-2" aria-hidden="true" tabindex="-1"></a><span class="cf">try</span>: </span> <span id="cb575-3"><a href="#cb575-3" aria-hidden="true" tabindex="-1"></a> inverse <span class="op">=</span> <span class="fl">1.0</span><span class="op">/</span><span class="bu">int</span>(a) </span> <span id="cb575-4"><a href="#cb575-4" aria-hidden="true" tabindex="-1"></a><span class="cf">except</span> <span class="pp">ZeroDivisionError</span>: </span> <span id="cb575-5"><a href="#cb575-5" aria-hidden="true" tabindex="-1"></a> <span class="bu">print</span>(<span class="st">"zero can't be inverted"</span>) </span></code></pre></div> <p>except, else, and finally</p> <p>Whenever the according kind of error occurs within a try clause, the except code block will be executed without breaking the program execution.</p> <p>Typical exceptions include the following:</p> <ul> <li>ZeroDivisionError: when dividing by zero<br /> </li> <li>KeyError: a key in a dictionary does not exist<br /> </li> <li>ValueError: a type conversion failed<br /> </li> <li>IOError: a file could not be opened</li> </ul> </section> <section id="what-exceptions-to-catch" class="level1"> <h1>What Exceptions to Catch</h1> <p>Generally, everything that can go wrong even though a program has been written with great diligence is worth catching. For example,</p> <ul> <li>File operations<br /> </li> <li>Web operations<br /> </li> <li>Database operations<br /> </li> <li>User input, especially numbers</li> </ul> <p>While it is possible to wrap an entire program into a single try… except clause, this often makes little sense, because the code gets less transparent and more difficult to debug.</p> </section> <section id="interrupting-program-execution" class="level1"> <h1>Interrupting Program Execution</h1> <p>You can interrupt the program execution and start the debugger by inserting these commands at any point:</p> <div class="sourceCode" id="cb576"><pre class="sourceCode python"><code class="sourceCode python"><span id="cb576-1"><a href="#cb576-1" aria-hidden="true" tabindex="-1"></a><span class="im">import</span> pdb </span> <span id="cb576-2"><a href="#cb576-2" aria-hidden="true" tabindex="-1"></a>pdb.set_trace() </span></code></pre></div> <p>The debugger shows a shell that works like the normal Python command line, but with some extra commands:</p> <ul> <li>n (next): execute next statement<br /> </li> <li>s (step): execute next statement and descend into functions<br /> </li> <li>l (list): show source code<br /> </li> <li>c (continue): continue execution until the next breakpoint<br /> </li> <li>help: print help message<br /> </li> <li>q (quit): abort the program</li> </ul> </section> <section id="breakpoints" class="level1"> <h1>Breakpoints</h1> <ul> <li>The command ‘b <line number>’ sets a breakpoint at the given line.<br /> </li> <li>The command ‘b’ displays all breakpoints set.</li> </ul> </section> <section id="a.2.19-comments" class="level1"> <h1>A.2.19 Comments</h1> <p>In Python, lines can be commented by the hash symbol (#) or by triple quotes. Python uses the triple quoted comments to generate documentation automatically.</p> <div class="sourceCode" id="cb577"><pre class="sourceCode txt"><code class="sourceCode default"><span id="cb577-1"><a href="#cb577-1" aria-hidden="true" tabindex="-1"></a># comment</span> <span id="cb577-2"><a href="#cb577-2" aria-hidden="true" tabindex="-1"></a>b = a/2.0 # half distance</span> <span id="cb577-3"><a href="#cb577-3" aria-hidden="true" tabindex="-1"></a>""This program</span> <span id="cb577-4"><a href="#cb577-4" aria-hidden="true" tabindex="-1"></a>calculates fruit prices. ""</span> <span id="cb577-5"><a href="#cb577-5" aria-hidden="true" tabindex="-1"></a>''''This program</span> <span id="cb577-6"><a href="#cb577-6" aria-hidden="true" tabindex="-1"></a>calculates fruit prices.''</span></code></pre></div> <p>Single commented line Comment after regular command Multiline comment enclosed by triple quotes Multiline comment enclosed by triple single quotes</p> <pre><code> # Appendix B: Python Resources # B.1 PYTHON DOCUMENTATION For general questions on Python, you will find these web links to be useful. * Python Homepage www.python.org This is the official Python website. * Python Tutorial http://docs.python.org/tut/tut.html This site provides a long tutorial for learning Python from scratch. Some chapters will be useful to help you thoroughly understand a specific topic. Use this if you don’t know anything about a particular topic. * The Python Standard Library http://docs.python.org/lib/lib.html The standard library is good for quickly finding a particular piece of information. Use this if you have an idea of what you are looking for but don’t remember how it is spelled in Python. * Global Module Index http://docs.python.org/2.7/py-modindex. html This is the documentation of all modules that come with Python. Look here if you know which module you want to use but don’t know how it works. * Python Tutorials http://awaretek.com/tutorials.html This is a large and exhaustive collection of Python tutorials covering most of the language aspects. It’s for beginners, advanced users, and experienced developers. # B.2 PYTHON COURSES * Instant Python www.hetland.org/python/instant-python.php This is a short and straight crash course in the programming language Python. * Think Python www.greenteapress.com/thinkpython/ Here you can download Think Python, which is an introduction to Python programming for beginners. It is a Free Book, which you are free to copy, distribute, and modify, as long as you attribute the work and don’t use it for commercial purposes. * Website Programming Applications IV (Python) Information http://bcu.copsewood.net/python/ Here you can find a 12-week Python course with notes and tutorial exercises. * Uta Priss’s Python Course www.upriss.org.uk/python/PythonCourse. html Here you will find a set of Python exercises with answers grouped by topics. * Python Short Course www.wag.caltech.edu/home/rpm/python_ course/ This is a short, nice Python course provided by Richard P. Muller (California Institute of Technology) in .ppt, .pdf, and .html formats. * Dive into Python www.diveintopython.net Dive into Python is a free Python book for experienced programmers. * Software Carpentry http://software-carpentry.org This is a programming course for scientists. It has been curated since 1998. # B.3 BIOPYTHON DOCUMENTATION * Biopython Home Page http://biopython.org/wiki/Main_Page This is the official website of Biopython. * Biopython Installation http://biopython.org/DIST/docs/install/ Installation.html Here you can find instructions on how to install Biopython on any operating system. * Getting Started http://biopython.org/wiki/Getting_Started Here you can find basic information to get started with Biopython. * Biopython Documentation http://biopython.org/wiki/Documentation This is a collection of links and references for Biopython beginners and advanced users. * Biopython Tutorial and Cookbook http://biopython.org/DIST/ docs/tutorial/Tutorial.html This site provides a long tutorial for learning Biopython from scratch. * Biopython Cookbook http://biopython.org/wiki/Category:Cookbook Here you can find a (growing) list of Biopython working examples (in alphabetical order) that are not present in the Biopython tutorial. # B.4 OTHER PYTHON LIBRARIES * Matplotlib http://matplotlib.org/ This powerful library for creating diagrams is part of the Scientific Python library (SciPy). * Python Imaging Library (PIL) www.pythonware.com/products/ pil/ PIL is a versatile library for manipulating images. * PyCogent http://pycogent.org/ PyCogent is a biological library with many functions similar to those of Biopython. However, its main strengths are handling RNA and phylogenetic analyses. * ModeRNA http://iimcb.genesilico.pl/moderna/ ModeRNA is a Python library to examine, manipulate, and build 3D models of RNA structures. # B.5 MOLECULAR VISUALIZATION SYSTEMS WRITTEN IN PYTHON * PyMOL www.pymol.org PyMOL is an open-source, user-sponsored, molecular visualization system. It can produce high-quality 3D images of molecules. * UCSF Chimera www.cgl.ucsf.edu/chimera/ UCSF Chimera is a highly extensible program for interactive visualization and analysis of molecular structures and related data. Highquality images and animations can be generated. # B.6 VIDEOS * Videos Tagged with Python (from Best Tech Videos) www.bestechvideos.com/tag/python/ This is a collection of handpicked technical videos tagged with Python dedicated to finding the best educational content for developers, designers, managers, and other people in IT. * The pydb Debugger www.bestechvideos.com/2006/12/16/introducing-the-pydb-debugger/ This is a live demo showing how the pydb debugger works. # B.7 GENERAL PROGRAMMING * Learning to Program www.freenetpages.co.uk/hp/alan.gauld/ This is a general guide for absolute beginners to programming. The majority of the course is written in Python even though, in principle, it can be easily applied to any programming language. # Appendix C: Record Samples # C.1 A SINGLE PROTEIN SEQUENCE FILE IN FASTA FORMAT ```html >sp|P03372|ESR1_HUMAN Estrogen receptor OS = Homo sapiens GN = ESR1 PE = 1 SV = 2 MTMLHTKASGMALLHQIQGNELEPLNRPQLIPLERPLGEVYLDDSSKPAVNYYPEGAAY EFAAAAAANAQVYGQTGLPYGPGEAAAFGSNGLGGFPLNSVSPSPLMLLHPPPQLSPFL QPHGQQVPYYLENEPGPSYTVREAGPPAFYRPNSNRQGGGRELASTNDKGSMAMESAK ETRYCAVCNDYASGYHYGVWSCEGCKAFFKRSIQGHNDYMCPATNQCTIDKNRRKSCQAC RLRKCYEVMKKGIRKDRRGGRMLKHKRQRDDGEGRGEVGSAGDMRAANLWPSLMIKR SKKNSLALSLTADQMVSALLDAEPPILSEYDPTRPFSEASMMGLTTNLADRELVHMINW AKRVPGFVDTLHDQVHLLECAWLEILMIGLVWRSMEHGKLLFAPNLLLLDRNQGKCVEG MVEIFDMLLATSSSRFRMMLNQGEEFVCLKSIILLNSGVYTFLSSTLKSEEKDHIRVLD KITDTLIHLMAKAGTLQQHQRLAQLLLILSHIRHMSNKGMEHLYSMKCKNVPLYDLL LELMDAHRLHAPTSRGASVEETDQSHLATAGSTSSHSLQKYYITGEAEGFPATV </code></pre> </section> <section id="c.2-a-single-nucleotide-sequence-file-in-fastaformat" class="level1"> <h1>C.2 A SINGLE NUCLEOTIDE SEQUENCE FILE IN FASTAFORMAT</h1> <div class="sourceCode" id="cb579"><pre class="sourceCode html"><code class="sourceCode html"><span id="cb579-1"><a href="#cb579-1" aria-hidden="true" tabindex="-1"></a>>ENSG00000188536|hemoglobin alpha 2 ATGGTGCTGTCTCCTGCCGACAAGACCAACGTCAAGGCCGCCTGGGGTAAGGTCGGCGCGCACGCT GGCGAGTATGGTGCGGAGGCCCTGGAGAGGATGTTCCTGTCCTTCCCCACCACCAAGACCTACTTC CCGCACTTCGACCTGAGCCACGGCTCTGCCCAGGTTAAGGCCACGGCAAGAAGGTGGCCGACGCG GCCCGCCCACCTCCCCGCCGAGTTCACCCCTGCGGTGCACGCCTCCCTGGACAAGTTCCTGGCTTCT GTGAGCACCGTGCTGACCTCCAAATACCGTTAA </span></code></pre></div> </section> <section id="c.3-an-example-of-an-rna-sequence-in-fasta-format" class="level1"> <h1>C.3 AN EXAMPLE OF AN RNA SEQUENCE IN FASTA FORMAT</h1> <blockquote> <p>ENSG00000188536|hemoglobin alpha 2 </td> <td> AY810830 </td> <td> 705 bp </td> <td> mRNA </td> <td> linear </td> <td> HTC 22-JUN-2006 </td> </tr> <tr> <td> DEFINITION </td> <td colspan="5"> Schistosoma japonicumSJCHGC07869 protein mRNA, partial cds. </td> </tr> <tr> <td> ACCSSION </td> <td colspan="5"> AY810830 </td> </tr> <tr> <td> VERSION </td> <td colspan="5"> AY810830.1 GI:60600350 </td> </tr> <tr> <td> KEYWORDS </td> <td colspan="5"> Eukaryota; Metazoa; Platyhelminthes; Trematoda; Digenea; Strigeida; Schistosomatoidea; Schistosomatidae; Schistosoma. </td> </tr> <tr> <td> REFERENCE </td> <td colspan="5"> 1 (bases 1 to 705) </td> </tr> <tr> <td> AUTHORS </td> <td colspan="5"> Liu,F., Lu,J., Hu,W., Wang,S.Y., Cui,S.J., Chi,M., Yan,Q., Wang,X.R., Song,H.D., Xu,X.N., Wang,J.J., Zhang,X.L., Zhang,X., Wang,Z.Q., transcriptomic and proteomic analyses of Schistosoma japonicum </td> </tr> <tr> <td> JOURNAL </td> <td colspan="5"> PLoS Pathog. 2 (4), E29 (2006) </td> </tr> <tr> <td> PUBMED </td> <td colspan="5"> OF A PDB FILE HEADER (PARTIAL)</p> <pre class="proteindb"><code>HEADER ADENYLATE KINASE 28-JUL-95 3AKY TITLE STABILITY, ACTIVITY AND STRUCTURE OF ADENYLATE KINASE TITLE 2 MUTANTS COMPND MOL_ID: 1; COMPND 2 MOLECULE: ADENYLATE KINASE; COMPND 3 CHAIN: A; COMPND 4 SYNONYM: ATP\AMP PHOSPHOTRANSFERASE, MYOKINASE; COMPND 5 EC: 2.7.4.3; COMPND 6 ENGINEERED: YES; COMPND 7 MUTATION: YES SOURCE MOL_ID: 1; SOURCE 2 ORGANISM_SCIENTIFIC: SACCHAROMYCES CEREVISIAE; SOURCE 3 ORGANISM_COMMON: BAKER'S YEAST; SOURCE 4 ORGANISM_TAXID: 4932; SOURCE 5 EXPRESSION_SYSTEM: ESCHERICHIA COLI; SOURCE 6 EXPRESSION_SYSTEM_TAXID: 562; SOURCE 7 EXPRESSION_SYSTEM_PLASMID: PUAKY KEYWDS ATP: AMP PHOSPHOTRANSFERASE, MYOKINASE, ADENYLATE KINASE EXPDTA X-RAY DIFFRACTION AUTHOR U.ABELE, G.E.SCHULZ REVDAT 2 24-FEB-09 3AKY 1 VERSN REVDAT 1 14-NOV-95 3AKY 0 JRNL AUTH P.SPUERGIN, U.ABELE, G.E.SCHULZ JRNL TITL STABILITY, ACTIVITY AND STRUCTURE OF ADENYLATE JRNL TITL 2 KINASE MUTANTS. JRNL REF EUR.J.BIOCHEM. V. 231 405 1995 JRNL REFN ISSN 0014-2956 JRNL PMID 7635152 JRNL DOI 10.1111/J.1432-1033.1995.TB20713.X REMARK 1 REMARK 1 REFERENCE 1 REMARK 1 AUTH U.ABELE, G.E.SCHULZ REMARK 1 TITL HIGH-RESOLUTION STRUCTURES OF ADENYLATE KINASE REMARK 1 TITL 2 FROM YEAST LIGATED WITH INHIBITOR AP5A, SHOWING REMARK 1 TITL 3 THE PATHWAY OF PHOSPHORYL TRANSFER REMARK 1 REF PROTEIN SCI. id="c.9-an-example-of-the-cuffcompare-output-for-three-samples-q1-q2-and-q3" class="level1"> <h1>C.9 AN EXAMPLE OF THE CUFFCOMPARE OUTPUT FOR THREE SAMPLES (Q1, Q2, AND Q3)</h1> <p>Because of the length of each line, the first field of each new line is in bold, and different lines are highlighted using indentation.</p> <pre class="csv"><code>Medullo-Diff_0000001 XLOC_000001 Lypla1|uc007afh.1
